steps.title: Use the dating conflict
steps.content: The single origin hypothesis says iron metallurgy began in Anatolia around 2200-2000 BCE and then spread elsewhere, including to Africa. But the Central African sites at Oboui and Gbatoro may date to 2300-1900 BCE, overlapping with or even predating the Anatolian evidence. A logical completion must account for Central African ironworking that is just as early as the proposed Anatolian origin.

steps.title: Avoid transmission claims
steps.content: A merely pushes Anatolia earlier even though the passage provides new evidence from Central Africa, not older evidence from Anatolia. B still assumes transmission from Anatolia to Central Africa by some other route, which the overlapping dates do not require. C splits the sites into different origins without support from the text. D explains the evidence by allowing iron production to have developed independently in Anatolia and Central Africa at about the same time. The answer is D.
